title: FlowRL: A Taxonomy and Modular Framework for Reinforcement Learning with Diffusion Policies
abstract: Thanks to their remarkable flexibility, diffusion models and flow models have emerged as promising candidates for policy representation. However, efficient reinforcement learning (RL) upon these policies remains a challenge due to the lack of explicit log-probabilities for vanilla policy gradient estimators. While numerous attempts have been proposed to address this, the field lacks a unified perspective to reconcile these seemingly disparate methods, thus hampering ongoing development. In this paper, we bridge this gap by introducing a comprehensive taxonomy for RL algorithms with diffusion/flow policies. To support reproducibility and agile prototyping, we introduce a modular, JAX-based open-source codebase that leverages JIT-compilation for high-throughput training. Finally, we provide systematic and standardized benchmarks across Gym-Locomotion, DeepMind Control Suite, and IsaacLab, offering a rigorous side-by-side comparison of diffusion-based methods and guidance for practitioners to choose proper algorithms based on the application. Our work establishes a clear foundation for understanding and algorithm design, a high-efficiency toolkit for future research in the field, and an algorithmic guideline for practitioners in generative models and robotics. Our code is available at https://github.com/typoverflow/flow-rl.

sections.heading: 1Introduction
sections.text:
Deep Reinforcement Learning (RL) has traditionally relied on simple distributions ( sac ; td3 ; sacd ) , such as the diagonal Gaussian distribution or Dirac delta distribution for policy parameterization. The appeal of such simple distributions lies in their mathematical convenience: they permit easy log \log -probability computation, rapid sampling, and tractable reparameterization, making them compatible with various optimization paradigms. Despite these computational benefits, simple distributions often fail to capture complex and multi-modal action distributions encountered in high-dimensional control ( dql ; idql ; sfbc ) . This limitation becomes increasingly evident in recent work, when more efforts are dedicated to training generalist policies that are capable of capturing diverse human behaviors ( black2024pi0 ; kim2024openvla ) .

Recently, diffusion models (DMs) ( ho2020denoising ; song2020score ) and flow models (FMs) ( albergo2025stochastic ; lipman2022flow ) have emerged as powerful alternatives for policy representation ( dql ; sfbc ) . They both employ an iterative sampling process and therefore offer greater flexibility in distribution modeling. However, integrating DMs and FMs into the RL optimization loop is non-trivial. Traditional RL workflows rely on policy gradient ( ppo ; trpo ) or the reparameterization trick ( td3 ) , both of which are notoriously difficult for DMs and FMs ( song2020score ) .
While in RL, the target action distribution is typically defined implicitly through the utilities measured by value functions or return functions ( haarnoja2017reinforcement ; peng2019advantage ) , from which direct samples are not available ( pan2024model ) , making vanilla diffusion/flow model training losses intractable.

To address these challenges, recent literature has proposed various solutions for diffusion policy-based Reinforcement Learning (DPRL). Existing methods span disparate scenarios, including offline ( dql ; dac ; bdpo ) , online ( qsm ; dacer ; sdac ) , and offline-to-online RL ( huang2025offline ) . Besides, they often involve confounding factors such as differences in noise schedules ( chen2023importance ) , network architectures ( idql ; dime ; nauman2024bigger ; lee2024simba ) , and evaluation protocols. These discrepancies make it difficult to isolate the true drivers of the algorithmic performance.

Therefore, in this paper, we aim to systematize the landscape of DPRL, providing a unified perspective to study these methods. Specifically, our contributions are threefold:

1) We summarize and categorize modern DPRL algorithms based on their guidance mechanism and choice of reference policy. This taxonomy allows us to study these methods from first principles and expose the underlying mathematical relationships between them.

2) Leveraging JIT-compilation provided by JAX ( jax2018github ) and its ecosystem ( deepmind2020jax ; flax2020github ) , we provide a modular, open-source codebase for representative DPRL algorithms with high-throughput training and inference. Furthermore, the library’s modular design allows researchers to swap environments and algorithmic components with minimal effort, significantly reducing the migration cost and barrier for RL research.

3) Based on our systematic taxonomy and high-performance algorithm library, we conduct a large-scale comparative study of DPRL algorithms across three diverse continuous control suites: Gym-Locomotion, DeepMind Control (DMC), and IsaacLab. Our results establish rigorous baselines and provide practitioners with actionable insights tailored to specific application requirements.

Comparison to existing surveys. Several surveys examine diffusion models in RL from different angles. zhu2023diffusion and xu2025diffusion provide broad taxonomies of diffusion roles (planners, synthesizers, policies) but lack a deep dive into the underlying policy optimization process. wolf2025diffusion and li2025generative shift the focus on specific applications like robotics and broader decision-making tasks. The closest work to ours is choi2026review , which reviews online diffusion policy algorithms. However, their categorization focuses only on guidance mechanisms, whereas we additionally investigate DPRL methods with different regularization objectives, thereby providing a more comprehensive overview of DPRL across different settings.

sections.heading: 2Background
sections.text:
Reinforcement Learning is based on the framework of Markov Decision Process (MDP) ⟨ 𝒮 , 𝒜 , T , R , γ , d 0 ⟩ \langle\mathcal{S},\mathcal{A},T,R,\gamma,d_{0}angle ( sutton1998reinforcement ) , where 𝒮 \mathcal{S} is the state space, 𝒜 \mathcal{A} is the action space, T ​ ( s ′ | s , a ) T(s^{\prime}|s,a) denotes the transition function, R ​ ( s , a ) R(s,a) is a bounded reward function, γ \gamma is the discount factor and d 0 ​ ( s 0 ) d_{0}(s_{0}) denotes the initial state distribution. The agent aims to learn a policy π : 𝒮 → Δ ​ ( 𝒜 ) \pi:\mathcal{S}ightarrow\Delta(\mathcal{A}) to maximize the expected discounted return 𝔼 π ​ [ ∑ t = 0 ∞ γ t ​ R ​ ( s t , a t ) ] \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[\sum_{t=0}^{\infty}\gamma^{t}R(s_{t},a_{t})] . In online RL, the agent can interact with the environment to gather experiences for policy optimization, while in offline RL, the agent is provided with an offline dataset 𝒟 = { ( s t , a t , s t + 1 , r t ) } \mathcal{D}=\{(s_{t},a_{t},s_{t+1},r_{t})\} , where s t + 1 ∼ T ( ⋅ | s t , a t ) s_{t+1}\sim T(\cdot|s_{t},a_{t}) and r t = R ​ ( s t , a t ) ∈ ℝ r_{t}=R(s_{t},a_{t})\in\mathbb{R} . We define the value functions as the expected reward of executing a certain policy π \pi : Q π ​ ( s , a ) = 𝔼 π ​ [ ∑ t = 0 ∞ γ t ​ r t | s 0 = s , a 0 = a ] , V π ​ ( s ) = 𝔼 π ​ [ ∑ t = 0 ∞ γ t ​ r t | s 0 = s ] , \displaystyle	extstyle Q^{\pi}(s,a)=\mathbb{E}_{\pi}\left[\sum_{t=0}^{\infty}\gamma^{t}r_{t}|s_{0}=s,a_{0}=aight],V^{\pi}(s)=\mathbb{E}_{\pi}\left[\sum_{t=0}^{\infty}\gamma^{t}r_{t}|s_{0}=sight], (1) which satisfy the Bellman equation Q π ​ ( s , a ) = R ​ ( s , a ) + γ ​ 𝔼 s ′ ∼ T ​ [ V ​ ( s ′ ) ] . Q^{\pi}(s,a)=R(s,a)+\gamma\mathbb{E}_{s^{\prime}\sim T}\left[V(s^{\prime})ight]. (2) With value functions defined, we can define the policy optimization objective ( wu2019behavior ; geist2019theory ) : max π ∈ Π ⁡ 𝔼 π ​ [ Q π ​ ( s , a ) ] − λ ​ D KL ​ ( π ∥ ν ) , \displaystyle\max_{\pi\in\Pi}\ \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\left[Q^{\pi}(s,a)ight]-\lambda D_{m KL}{\left(\pi\|

The optimal solution to ( 3 ) can be obtained by the KKT conditions, i.e. , π ∗ ​ ( a | s ) ∝ ν ​ ( a | s ) ​ exp ⁡ ( Q ​ ( s , a ) / λ ) . \displaystyle\pi^{*}(a|s)\propto

Existing RL algorithms can be roughly categorized based on how the gradient is computed: 1), Policy Gradient methods ( sutton1999policy ; trpo ; ppo ) , which uses the reinforce trick to derive the gradient of ( 3 ) as ∇ θ ( 𝔼 π θ ​ [ Q ​ ( s , a ) ] − λ ​ D ​ ( π θ ∥ ν ) ) = 𝔼 π θ ​ [ Q ​ ( s , a ) ​ ∇ θ log ⁡ π θ ​ ( a | s ) ] − λ ​ ∇ θ D ​ ( π θ ∥ ν )

Diffusion Models (DMs) and Flow Models (FMs) approximate data distributions by gradually perturbing clean data x 0 ∼ q 0 = p data x^{0}\sim q_{0}=p_{m data} with isotropic Gaussian noise according to a forward process ( song2020score ; albergo2025stochastic ) : x ( t ) = α t ​ x ( 0 ) + σ t ​ ϵ , ϵ ∼ 𝒩 ​ ( 0 , I ) , x^{(t)}=\alpha_{t}x^{(0)}+\sigma_{t}\epsilon,\quad\epsilon\sim\mathcal{N}(0,I), (5) which emits the condition distribution q t | 0 ​ ( x ( t ) | x ( 0 ) ) = 𝒩 ​ ( x ( t ) ; α t ​ x ( 0 ) , σ t 2 ) q_{t|0}(x^{(t)}|x^{(0)})=\mathcal{N}(x^{(t)};\alpha_{t}x^{(0)},\sigma_{t}^{2}) . It then learns the score function s θ ​ ( x ( t ) , t ) s_{	heta}(x^{(t)},t) or velocity function v θ ​ ( x ( t ) , t ) v_{	heta}(x^{(t)},t) to reverse this process to sample from the target distribution p data p_{m data} . Since DMs and FMs mainly differ in the noise schedules ( α t , σ t ) (\alpha_{t},\sigma_{t}) and their network predictions s t s_{t} and v t v_{t} can be reparameterized interchangeably ( li2025back ; lu2024simplifying ) , we will discuss with DMs for simplicity, but we emphasize the proposed taxonomy can also be applicable to FMs.
The target for the score function is to match the marginal score field, s t ​ ( x ( t ) ) = 𝔼 x ( 0 ) ∼ p 0 | t ( ⋅ | x ( t ) ) ​ [ s t | 0 ​ ( x ( t ) | x ( 0 ) ) ] , \displaystyle s_{t}(x^{(t)})=\mathbb{E}_{x^{(0)}\sim p_{0|t}(\cdot|x^{(t)})}\left[s_{t|0}\left(x^{(t)}|x^{(0)}ight)ight], (6) where p 0 | t p_{0|t} is the posterior distribution defined via Bayes’ theorem as p 0 | t ​ ( x ( 0 ) | x ( t ) ) = q t | 0 ​ ( x ( t ) | x ( 0 ) ) ​ q 0 ​ ( x ( 0 ) ) / q t ​ ( x ( t ) ) p_{0|t}(x^{(0)}|x^{(t)})=q_{t|0}(x^{(t)}|x^{(0)})q_{0}(x^{(0)})/q_{t}(x^{(t)}) , and s t | 0 ​ ( x ( t ) | x ( 0 ) ) = ∇ x ( t ) log ⁡ p t | 0 ​ ( x ( t ) | x ( 0 ) ) = − ( x ( t ) − α t ​ x ( 0 ) ) / σ t 2 . s_{t|0}(x^{(t)}|x^{(0)})=
abla_{x^{(t)}}\log p_{t|0}(x^{(t)}|x^{(0)})=-(x^{(t)}-\alpha_{t}x^{(0)})/\sigma_{t}^{2}. (7) However, the posterior is not accessible. In practice, we employ conditional score matching , which also yields the same target score: ℒ ​ ( θ ) = 𝔼 x ( 0 ) , x ( t ) ​ [ ‖ s θ ​ ( x ( t ) , t ) − s t | 0 ​ ( x ( t ) , x ( 0 ) ) ‖ 2 ] . \displaystyle\mathcal{L}(	heta)=\mathbb{E}_{x^{(0)},x^{(t)}}\left[\|s_{	heta}(x^{(t)},t)-s_{t|0}(x^{(t)},x^{(0)})\|^{2}ight]. (8) After training, sampling is done by solving a stochastic or ordinary differential equation (SDE/ODE). For example, d ​ x ( t ) = [ α ˙ t α t ​ x ( t ) − σ t 2 ​ ( σ ˙ t σ t − α ˙ t α t ) ​ s θ ​ ( x ( t ) , t ) − η ​ s θ ​ ( x ( t ) , t ) ] ​ d ​ t + 2 ​ η ​ d ​ w ¯ , \displaystyle\mathrm{d}x^{(t)}=\left[ rac{\dot{\alpha}_{t}}{\alpha_{t}}x^{(t)}-\sigma_{t}^{2}\left( rac{\dot{\sigma}_{t}}{\sigma_{t}}- rac{\dot{\alpha}_{t}}{\alpha_{t}}ight)s_{	heta}(x^{(t)},t)-\eta s_{	heta}(x^{(t)},t)ight]\mathrm{d}t+\sqrt{2\eta}\mathrm{d} ar{w}, (9) where η ⩾ 0 \eta\geqslant 0 controls the stochasticity and w ¯  ar{w} is the Brownian motion in reverse time ( albergo2025stochastic ; albergo2022building ) .

Diffusion Policies ( chi2025diffusion ; dql ; edp ; dppo ) have been developed by exploiting conditional diffusion models for generating action a a conditioned on a given state s s . In this paper, we use π t \pi_{t} to denote the distribution of intermediate samples at diffusion time t t and k k to denote the environment time. Under this terminology, a k ( t ) a^{(t)}_{k} denotes the intermediate action sample at s k s_{k} and diffusion step t t .

sections.heading: 3Taxonomy of RL with Diffusion Policies
sections.text:
At its core, RL optimizes the regularized objective iteratively defined in ( 3 ), whose optimal policy takes the form π ​ ( a | s ) ∝ ν ​ ( a | s ) ​ exp ⁡ ( Q ​ ( s , a ) ) \pi(a|s)\propto
u(a|s)\exp(Q(s,a)) , where ν 
u is a reference policy and Q ​ ( s , a ) Q(s,a) is the value function from policy evaluation. The choice of ν ​ ( a | s ) 
u(a|s) determines the algorithm’s behavior across different learning paradigms: • Maximum Entropy RL : When ν = Unif ​ ( 𝒜 ) 
u=	ext{Unif}(\mathcal{A}) , the KL divergence reduces to the policy entropy up to some constant, which is a standard approach in online RL to encourage exploration; • Policy Mirror Descent : When ν = π k − 1 
u=\pi^{k-1} (the policy checkpoint from the last iteration), this ensures safe exploitation by constraining updates to the proximity of the current policy; • Behavior Regularized RL : When ν = π 𝒟 
u=\pi_{\mathcal{D}} (the dataset’s behavior policy), this restricts the policy to the support of the offline data 𝒟 \mathcal{D} , ensuring stable optimization in offline settings.

Consequently, implementing RL for diffusion policies requires addressing two concurrent challenges: 1. How to effectively guide diffusion policy optimization using the value function Q ​ ( s , a ) Q(s,a) , and 2. How to enforce proximity to the chosen reference policy ν 
u . Therefore, we examine existing literature through the lens of guidance method and reference policy , respectively. Table 1 provides a categorization of existing DPRL methods based on our taxonomy.

sections.heading: 3.1Best-of-N (BoN) Sampling
sections.text: The simplest approach is pretraining a diffusion policy to approximate the reference distribution ν ​ ( a | s ) \nu(a|s) and refining it at inference time via Best-of-N sampling: a ∗ = argmax a i ∈ { a 1 , … , a N } Q ( s , a i ) , where a i ∼ ν ( ⋅ | s ) . \displaystyle a^{*}=\mathop{\mathrm{argmax}}_{a_{i}\in\{a_{1},\dots,a_{N}\}}\ Q(s,a_{i}),\quad\text{where }a_{i}\sim\nu(\cdot|s). (10) This approach was first proposed in offline settings by sfbc and idql , where a diffusion model is trained to represent the offline data distribution and actions are subsequently refined using a learned critic Q ​ ( s , a ) Q(s,a) . Today, BoN sampling is increasingly used in the evaluation of modern DPRL methods. Because diffusion policies exhibit superior coverage across the action space, applying BoN sampling at inference enables the agent to select the highest-valued actions within the distribution, effectively trading additional inference-time computation for improved performance.

sections.heading: 3.2QQ-value Guidance
sections.text:
Drawing inspiration from classifier guidance ( dhariwal2021diffusion ) , sampling from a Q Q -weighted distribution π ∗ ​ ( a | s ) ∝ ν ​ ( a | s ) ​ exp ⁡ ( Q ​ ( s , a ) / λ ) \pi^{*}(a|s)\propto
u(a|s)\exp(Q(s,a)/\lambda) can be achieved by injecting action gradients ∇ a Q ​ ( s , a ) 
abla_{a}Q(s,a) into the sampling process. This is due to the following score function relationship: ∇ a log ⁡ π ∗ ​ ( a | s ) \displaystyle
abla_{a}\log\pi^{*}(a|s) = ∇ a log ⁡ ν ​ ( a | s ) + ∇ a Q ​ ( s , a ) / λ . \displaystyle=
abla_{a}\log
u(a|s)+
abla_{a}Q(s,a)/\lambda. (11) Some algorithms utilize an explicit policy optimization step to internalize this guidance, while others inject the action gradient directly during sampling. For example, in the offline RL setting, DAC ( dac ) performs diffusion matching over offline dataset samples combined with the action gradient; in online RL, where ν = Unif ​ ( 𝒜 ) 
u=	ext{Unif}(\mathcal{A}) , QSM ( qsm ) directly regresses the score network towards the action gradients.

Unfortunately, directly using the action gradient is biased due to imprecise score mixing at intermediate diffusion steps. The precise score of diffusion step t t is given by ∇ a ( t ) log ⁡ π t ∗ ​ ( a ( t ) | s ) = ∇ a ( t ) log ⁡ ν t ​ ( a | s ) + ∇ a ( t ) η ​ log ⁡ 𝔼 a ( 0 ) ∼ p 0 | t ​ [ exp ⁡ ( Q ​ ( s , a ( 0 ) ) / η ) ] ⏟ Q t ​ ( s , a ( t ) ) . \displaystyle
abla_{a^{(t)}}\log\pi^{*}_{t}(a^{(t)}|s)=
abla_{a^{(t)}}\log
u_{t}(a|s)+
abla_{a^{(t)}}\underbrace{\eta\log\mathbb{E}_{a^{(0)}\sim p_{0|t}}[\exp(Q(s,a^{(0)})/\eta)]}_{Q_{t}(s,a^{(t)})}. (12) QGPO ( qgpo ) first formalizes this mismatch in offline scenarios where ν = π 𝒟 
u=\pi_{\mathcal{D}} , and introduces a contrastive learning objective to construct the intermediate Q t Q_{t} -functions. Similarly, Diffusion-DICE ( diffusiondice ) leverages Gumbel regression to estimate the intermediate Q t Q_{t} -functions. In online scenarios where π ∗ ​ ( a | s ) ∝ exp ⁡ ( Q ​ ( s , a ) ) \pi^{*}(a|s)\propto\exp(Q(s,a)) , rather than learning the Q t Q_{t} -function explicitly, iDEM ( idem ) and DPS ( dps ) propose an importance-sampling approach to estimate the intermediate scores: ∇ a ( t ) log ⁡ π t ∗ ​ ( a ( t ) | s ) \displaystyle
abla_{a^{(t)}}\log\pi_{t}^{*}(a^{(t)}|s) = 𝔼 a ^ ( 0 ) ∼ p t | 0 ​ [ exp ⁡ ( Q ​ ( s , a ^ ( 0 ) ) ) ​ ∇ a ( t ) Q ​ ( s , a ^ ( 0 ) ) ] 𝔼 a ^ ( 0 ) ∼ p t | 0 ​ [ exp ⁡ ( Q ​ ( s , a ^ ( 0 ) ) ) ] \displaystyle= rac{\mathbb{E}_{\hat{a}^{(0)}\sim p_{t|0}}[\exp(Q(s,\hat{a}^{(0)}))
abla_{a^{(t)}}Q(s,\hat{a}^{(0)})]}{\mathbb{E}_{\hat{a}^{(0)}\sim p_{t|0}}[\exp(Q(s,\hat{a}^{(0)}))]} (13) where the RHS can be approximated by Monte-Carlo samples and reweighting. The resulting smoothing of the action gradients helps alleviate the slow mixing issue in Langevin-style sampling.

sections.heading: 3.3Reparameterization
sections.text:
Similar to reparameterization-based methods like SAC ( sac ) and TD3 ( td3 ) , recent work explores reparameterizing the diffusion sampling process, and optimizing the network by maximizing Q Q -values of the generated samples. A common approach is Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) ( dql ; dacer ; dacerv2 ; dime ) , which computes and backpropagates the gradients across the entire sampling chain: max θ ⁡ 𝔼 a ( 0 : T ) ∼ π θ ​ [ Q ​ ( s , a θ ( 0 ) ) ] . \displaystyle\max_{\theta}\ \mathbb{E}_{a^{(0:T)}\sim\pi_{\theta}}[Q(s,a^{(0)}_{\theta})]. (14) However, BPTT incurs significant memory and computational overhead due to the need to preserve the computational graph across all diffusion timesteps. To mitigate this, several methods aim to amortize or bypass the sampling cost. For example, BDPO ( bdpo ) constructs step-level value functions Q ​ ( s , a , t ) Q(s,a,t) for each diffusion step t t , and optimizes only for single-step transitions max θ ⁡ 𝔼 a ( t ) ∼ π t | t + 1 , θ ​ [ Q ​ ( s , a θ ( t ) , t ) ] \max_{\theta}\ \mathbb{E}_{a^{(t)}\sim\pi_{t|t+1,\theta}}[Q(s,a^{(t)}_{\theta},t)] . EDP ( edp ) leverages the posterior mean estimate a ^ ( 0 ) \hat{a}^{(0)} via the identity a ^ ( 0 ) = 𝔼 a ( 0 ) ∼ p 0 | t ​ [ a ( 0 ) ] ≈ ( a ( t ) + σ t 2 ​ ϵ θ ) / α t \hat{a}^{(0)}=\mathbb{E}_{a^{(0)}\sim p_{0|t}}[a^{(0)}]\approx(a^{(t)}+\sigma_{t}^{2}\epsilon_{\theta})/\alpha_{t} and turns to optimize an efficient yet biased objective: max θ ⁡ 𝔼 a ( t ) , a ^ θ ( 0 ) = ( a ( t ) + σ t 2 ​ ϵ θ ) / α t ​ [ Q ​ ( s , a ^ θ ( 0 ) ) ] . \displaystyle\max_{\theta}\ \mathbb{E}_{a^{(t)},\hat{a}^{(0)}_{\theta}=(a^{(t)}+\sigma_{t}^{2}\epsilon_{\theta})/\alpha_{t}}[Q(s,\hat{a}^{(0)}_{\theta})]. (15) Another line of research, exemplified by FQL ( fql ) , employs distillation to compress multi-step diffusion into single-step models, enabling efficient reparameterization in a single pass.

Nevertheless, a fundamental challenge for this guidance style is how to ensure the policy respects the regularization. Since the log \log -probability of actions is difficult to compute, these methods typically require additional loss terms for regularization. One approach is to sample from the reference distribution and apply a standard diffusion loss on these samples to anchor the policy. For example, D-QL ( dql ) applies a score matching objective on samples from the offline dataset 𝒟 \mathcal{D} to prevent the diffusion policy from deviating. Alternatively, BDPO formulates the KL constraint across the entire diffusion path and enforces the constraint at every denoising step by decomposing the pathwise KL. Similarly, DIME ( dime ) derives a tractable lower bound for action entropy, which enables optimizing the diffusion policy with the maximum entropy framework.

sections.heading: 3.4Weighted Matching
sections.text:
Weighted matching methods optimize diffusion policies by reframing policy improvement as a weighted supervised learning task. These methods reweight the diffusion training objective using functions derived from the Q Q -function or advantage function to bias the learned policy toward high-value actions. qipo and sdac demonstrate that the weighted score/flow matching objective yields an optimal policy that exactly matches the closed-form expression in ( 4 ), providing formal guarantees for policy improvement.

In its general form, the weighted matching objective is defined as: min θ 𝔼 a ( 0 ) , a ( t ) ∼ p ~ 0 , t [ exp ( Q ​ ( s , a ( 0 ) ) λ ) ∥ s θ ( a ( t ) ; s , t ) − s t | 0 ( a ( t ) | a ( 0 ) ) ∥ 2 ] , \min_{\theta}\ \mathbb{E}_{a^{(0)},a^{(t)}\sim{\color[rgb]{1,0,0}\definecolor[named]{pgfstrokecolor}{rgb}{1,0,0}\tilde{p}_{0,t}}}\left[\exp\left(\frac{Q(s,a^{(0)})}{\lambda}\right)\left\|s_{\theta}\left(a^{(t)};s,t\right)-s_{t|0}\left(a^{(t)}|a^{(0)}\right)\right\|^{2}\right], (16) where p ~ 0 , t \tilde{p}_{0,t} represents a proposal distribution for the coupling ( a ( 0 ) , a ( t ) ) (a^{(0)},a^{(t)}) , and the choice of this distribution varies by setting. In offline RL where ν = π 𝒟 \nu=\pi_{\mathcal{D}} , QIPO ( qipo ) proves that sampling a ( 0 ) ∼ 𝒟 , a ( t ) ∼ q t | 0 a^{(0)}\sim\mathcal{D},a^{(t)}\sim q_{t|0} recovers the optimal policy ( 4 ). For mirror descent where ν = π k − 1 \nu=\pi^{k-1} , methods such as DPMD ( sdac ) and FPMD ( fpmd ) sample a ( 0 ) ∼ π k − 1 a^{(0)}\sim\pi^{k-1} and a ( t ) ∼ q t | 0 a^{(t)}\sim q_{t|0} , which also recovers the optimal policy in ( 4 ). In online RL, QVPO ( QVPO ) samples a ( 0 ) a^{(0)} from a mixture of the uniform distribution and the last policy checkpoint; while SDAC ( sdac ) and MaxEntDP ( dong2025maximum ) concurrently propose reverse sampling , where a ( 0 ) a^{(0)} is drawn by reversing the forward perturbation kernel q t | 0 q_{t|0} given a ( t ) a^{(t)} .

sections.heading: 3.5Policy Gradient
sections.text:
Policy gradient methods optimize the policy by estimating the gradients of the objective in ( 3 ) via the policy gradient theorem. A prominent example is Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) ( ppo ) , which stabilizes training by applying proximal regularization relative to the previous iteration’s policy, π k − 1 \pi^{k-1} . The training objective is max θ ⁡ 𝔼 a ∼ π k − 1 ​ [ min ⁡ ( r ​ ( θ ) ​ A ^ , clip ​ ( r ​ ( θ ) , 1 − ϵ clip , 1 + ϵ clip ) ​ A ^ ) ] , \max_{\theta}\mathbb{E}_{a\sim\pi^{k-1}}\left[\min\left(r(\theta)\hat{A},\mathrm{clip}\left(r(\theta),1-\epsilon^{\mathrm{clip}},1+\epsilon^{\mathrm{clip}}\right)\hat{A}\right)\right], (17) where ϵ clip \epsilon^{\mathrm{clip}} is a clipping threshold to avoid large updates and r ​ ( θ ) = π θ ​ ( a | s ) π k − 1 ​ ( a | s ) r(\theta)=\frac{\pi_{\theta}\left(a|s\right)}{\pi^{k-1}\left(a|s\right)} is the policy ratio.

The primary obstacle in applying policy gradient methods to diffusion policies is that computing the ratio r ​ ( θ ) r(\theta) requires the exact log-likelihood log ⁡ π ​ ( a | s ) \log\pi(a|s) , which is intractable due to the iterative generation process. By discretizing the generative SDE, DPPO ( dppo ) treats the denoising process as a Markov Decision Process (MDP) where each transition step is tractable. This allows extending PPO directly to the denoising steps. GenPO ( genpo ) constructs an invertible diffusion model by introducing dummy actions and uses the change-of-variables theorem to compute the action likelihood. However, this is computationally expensive. Finally, FPO ( fpo ) employs the conditional flow matching (CFM) loss as an approximation of the evidence lower bound (ELBO), and estimates the policy ratio as: r ^ FPO ​ ( θ ) = exp ⁡ ( L ^ CFM , θ k − 1 ​ ( a ) − L ^ CFM , θ ​ ( a ) ) , \hat{r}_{\mathrm{FPO}}\left(\theta\right)=\exp\left(\hat{L}_{\mathrm{CFM},\theta^{k-1}}\left(a\right)-\hat{L}_{\mathrm{CFM},\theta}\left(a\right)\right), (18) where the CFM loss is estimated via Monte Carlo samples: L ^ CFM , θ ​ ( a ) = 1 N ​ ∑ i = 1 N ‖ s θ ​ ( a ( t ) , t ) − s t | 0 ​ ( a ( t ) , a ( 0 ) ) ‖ 2 . \hat{L}_{\mathrm{CFM},\theta}\left(a\right)=\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left\|s_{\theta}\left(a^{(t)},t\right)-s_{t|0}\left(a^{(t)},a^{(0)}\right)\right\|^{2}. (19)

sections.heading: 4.1Design Principles
sections.text:
To support the large-scale comparative study and facilitate future research, our codebase, FlowRL , is designed according to the following principles:

Modularity and Composability. The library decouples RL algorithms into orthogonal, reusable components, such as neural network architectures, actor/critic variants, generative models (flow and diffusion), and training infrastructure. Each component adheres to a shared interface, allowing researchers to combine them flexibly.

Computational Efficiency. FlowRL is built around JAX and its ecosystem. Since diffusion models require multiple iterations to sample a single action, JAX’s Just-In-Time compilation provides a significant speedup in training and inference over PyTorch-based alternatives. Additionally, JAX’s functional paradigm enables the native use of transformations like vmap (used, e.g., for critic ensembles) and lax.scan (for the iterative
denoising loop in diffusion).

sections.heading: 4.2Architecture Overview
sections.text: Figure 1 provides a semantic overview of FlowRL . The system is organized into four layers, described below from bottom to top.

sections.heading: 6Closing Remarks
sections.text: In this paper, we presented a systematic study of diffusion policy-based Reinforcement Learning. We first proposed a unified taxonomy that categorizes existing DPRL algorithms along two axes: diffusion policy optimization guidance and regularization objective. Building upon this taxonomy, we developed a modular, JAX-based library that enables high-throughput training and fair comparison of DPRL algorithms. Our standardized benchmark results across Gym-Locomotion, DeepMind Control Suite, and IsaacLab provide rigorous performance references and practical guidelines for selecting algorithm configurations. Important open questions remain in the field of DPRL, including designing algorithms robust to diverse environment characteristics, scaling to long-horizon and sparse-reward tasks, and developing a thorough understanding of the diffusion policy optimization landscape. We believe our proposed taxonomy and library provide a solid foundation for future research and practical applications in this direction.

sections.heading: A.1Benchmark Tasks
sections.text: We evaluate on the following continuous control tasks across three benchmarks.

figures.caption:
Figure 2 : Representative tasks across three continuous-control suites.
Left: MuJoCo Gymnasium.
Middle: DeepMind Control Suite.
Right: IsaacLab.
Figure 3 : Performance profile on Gym-Locomotion tasks.
Figure 4 : Training curves of several off-policy DPRL algorithms across Gym-Locomotion tasks.
Figure 5 : Training curves of on-policy DPRL algorithms on IsaacLab tasks.
Figure 6 : Effect of Action Dimensionality. Performance is normalized by SAC across tasks of increasing action dimension.

Figure 7 : Effect of diffusion steps on performance. Performance of QSM, SDAC, and DACER when varying the number of diffusion steps (2, 5, 10, and 50).
Returns are normalized by task-specific maximum scores and averaged across Ant, HalfCheetah, Humanoid, and Walker2d.

Figure 8 : Effect of network architecture. Comparison between MLP and SimBa backbones for QSM (blue) and DACER (green) on DMControl hard tasks. Solid lines denote MLP policies and dashed lines denote Simba policies.
Figure 9 : Effect of noise schedule. Performance of SDAC, QSM, and DACER with a cosine (black) or linear noise schedule.
